title: HTML Entities
content:
### HTML Entities **Entities** are special codes for displaying reserved or invisible characters. --- ### Why Entities? Some characters have special meaning in HTML: - `<` and `>` are used for tags - `&` starts an entity - `"` is used for attributes To display them, use entities. --- ### Common Entities | Character | Entity Name | Entity Number | |-----------|-------------|---------------| | `<` | `<` | `<` | | `>` | `>` | `>` | | `&` | `&` | `&` | | `"` | `"` | `"` | | `'` | `'` (not in HTML4) | `'` | | ` ` (non-breaking space) | ` ` | ` ` | | `©` | `©` | `©` | | `®` | `®` | `®` | | `™` | `™` | `™` | | `€` | `€` | `€` | | `£` | `£` | `£` | | `¥` | `¥` | `¥` | --- ### Using Entities ```html

10 < 20 is true

Use & for ampersands

© 2024 My Company

Save & Exit

Non-breaking space

``` --- ### Non-Breaking Space (` `) Prevents line breaks between words: ```html

10 km/h

``` --- ### Combining Diacritical Marks ```html

À = A with grave (A + ̀)

ö = o with umlaut (o + ̈)

``` --- > **Best Practice:** Always use `<` and `>` when displaying code examples in HTML. Use `&` for ampersands in URLs and text.
codeExample:
Use < and > for tags in text.

Ampersand: &

Copyright: © 2024

Trademark: ™

Euro: € 100

Non-breaking space: Hello World

Math: 10 < 20 && 20 > 10
